mechanism_summary: Hospital rescue bias falls only when upstream access becomes visible, funded and politically salient.
Management attention shifts upstream when primary and ambulance outcomes have hospital-equivalent salience.
Additional contacts expand only when combined public benefit and co-payment exceed marginal cost, admin cost and risk.
Patients move to lower-cost access when price, wait, travel and trust costs are lower than delay or ED substitution.
Direct claiming improves entry only if PHO/locality equity and population-health functions are explicitly preserved.
ACC activity funding can stabilise lower-cost capacity; constraining it in isolation risks spillover to Health NZ and patients.
Alternative disposition is stable only when payment, clinical governance, data and follow-up reduce organisational risk.
Funding eligibility should follow safe scope and governance, not professional category alone.
Telehealth improves simple access but must be integrated and paired with local in-person capacity.
Co-payment can moderate discretionary demand but becomes a delayed-care mechanism without protections.
